scenario: Lattice Insurance maintains a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table that evaluates 12000 claims per week. Several rules may match a single claim for a given insurer customer, and a new developer on the team wants to understand exactly what the COLLECT hit policy returns when no aggregator is configured on the decisionTable XML element.
question: What does the COLLECT hit policy return for a DMN decision table when no aggregator is specified on the decisionTable XML element?

options.text: Returns a single output produced by summing every satisfied rule's output into one number, regardless of any aggregator setting
Returns the output of only the first satisfied rule and ignores the other rules that also match the inputs for that case
Returns a list of outputs from all satisfied rules in an arbitrary order with no defined ordering between the list entries
Returns a list of outputs from all satisfied rules ordered by their position in the decision table so the sequence is stable

a.text: Incorrect. SUM is an optional aggregator on the COLLECT hit policy, but COLLECT without any aggregator does not implicitly sum the outputs; the docs describe the no-aggregator case as returning a list of outputs in arbitrary order.

b.text: Incorrect. Returning only the first matched rule's output is the documented behaviour of the FIRST hit policy, not COLLECT; COLLECT returns the outputs of every satisfied rule rather than just the first one.

c.text: Correct. The DMN docs state that COLLECT returns the output of all satisfied rules as a list in an arbitrary order, and the page explicitly notes that the list produced by this hit policy has no defined ordering.

d.text: Incorrect. Ordering by rule position is the behaviour of the RULE ORDER hit policy; the docs explicitly note that the COLLECT output list has no ordering between its entries.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 DMN docs define COLLECT without an aggregator as returning a list of all satisfied rule outputs in arbitrary order. The page states the list has no ordering, so consumers cannot rely on any deterministic sequence of entries.
